stem: A particle is moved along the curve \( \mathbf{r}(t) = (t,\; t^{2}) \), \( 0 \le t \le 1 \), by the force field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = ( ([[a]])y,\; ([[b]])x ) \). Compute the work \( \displaystyle W = \int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: On the path \( x=t,\ y=t^{2} \), so \( \mathbf{r}'(t)=(1,2t) \) and \( \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r}(t))=([[a]]t^{2},[[b]]t) \). Then \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{r}' = ([[a]]+2([[b]]))t^{2} \) and \( W=\int_0^1 ([[a]]+2([[b]]))t^{2}\,dt = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[a]] + 2[[b]] \), the integrand's coefficient; it forgets the factor \( \int_0^1 t^{2}\,dt = \tfrac{1}{3} \).
This dots the fields incorrectly, adding the raw coefficients instead of forming \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{r}' \).
This has the sign of the work reversed (path traversed backwards).
This is off by one from the correct work.
This is off by one from the correct work.
This is off by two from the correct work.

stem: A particle moves along the space curve \( \mathbf{r}(t) = (t,\; t^{2},\; t^{3}) \), \( 0 \le t \le 1 \), through the force field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = ( ([[a]])y,\; ([[b]])z,\; ([[c]])x ) \). Compute the work \( \displaystyle W = \int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the raw coefficients \( a+b+c \), skipping the dot product with \( \mathbf{r}'(t) \) and the integration entirely.
This has the coefficient on the \( a \)-term wrong (should be \( a/3 \), not swapped with the b/c pattern) from mismatching which power integrates to which fraction.
This has the sign of the work reversed (path traversed backwards).
This assumes every term integrates to a factor of \( 1/2 \), ignoring the different powers of \( t \) involved.
This is off by one from the correct work.
This is off by one from the correct work.

stem: A particle moves once counterclockwise around the full circle \( \mathbf{r}(t) = ([[R]]\cos t,\; [[R]]\sin t) \), \( 0 \le t \le 2\pi \), through the rotational force field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = ( -([[a]])y,\; ([[a]])x ) \). Compute the work \( \displaystyle W = \int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes a rotational field does zero work around a closed loop, like a conservative field would; this field is NOT conservative.
This forgets to multiply by the \( 2\pi \) from integrating the constant integrand over the full loop.
This has the wrong sign and only a factor of \( \pi \) instead of \( 2\pi \).
This uses only \( \pi \) instead of \( 2\pi \), as if the particle traversed just a half-circle.
This uses \( 4\pi \), as if the particle went around the loop twice.
This adds a spurious \( \pi \) to the correct value.
